paragraphs.text: The story briefly is this. Each fact corroberated personally by a research covering thirty years.
A woman living alone on one of the Santa Barbara Islands, for over eighteen years, was found by a party of seal hunters, and brought to Santa Barbara in 1853.
She died shortly after, and her country has never been established.

It has been supposed that she was an Indian from the fact that thirty Indians, comprising the total population, had been removed from the Island in 1835 [...] and brought to San Gabriel, to work in the mission be instructed by the Mission Fathers. One of the residents A woman jumped overboard during the confusion of embarking and returned to the Island[.]

The storm necessitated the her [...] at that time, and [...] (the only one on the coast) [...] [...] after landing [...] [...] the next trip to San Francisco thereby preventing the intended search for the missing woman [...] for fifteen years—no boat could be secured to take the rescue journey of seventy miles. out of the [...] of traffic The gold and excitement brought sailors and craft to California, so that and in 1851, a mission father of Santa Barbara, by the payment of two hundred dollars, induced succeeded in sending a [...] craft to search the island. The father had never ceased his prayers that the abandoned one might be preserved until help reached her—but as year after year passed, the probability of her death, was accepted by all those who knew the circumstances. [...] The Captain reported that he found no trace of occupation of San Nicholas, and thus the matter was settled. His reports of fine otter fishing in the vicinity of this [...] island, and [...] [...] unknown island, [...] our Captain Nidever of Santa Barbara, to go with his own boat and a crew of [...] Indians the following year as an otter hunt. Their camp was made on the beach, and they worked busily for six weeks, secured a cargo of pelts, and in the midst of a at the approach of a [...] storm, they made haste to shelter in the lee of San Miguel. The sailors reported that from the [...], a woman ran up and down the along the edge of a cliff, in the pelting storm, and beconed beckoned as they left anchorage—the sheets of rain hid her from plain view—It was supposed to be the ghost of the woman who perished there.

The following year the boat went again to the otter grounds.—Camped in the same spot. Having no occasion to explore the precipitous heights of the island—they [...] thus remained close to shore, and as they

There is a doubt in my own mind, whether the woman was Indian. There is a rumor that a very short time before the Island was depopulated, a woman was cast ashore from a wreck, and that shortly after she gave birth to a child. This information has reached me since the material was gathered for my article as originally published. The search for facts, was undertaken with by four persons [...]Dr[.] Dimmick[,] Henry Chapman[, ...] artist, and James Calkins, [...] all three residing in Santa Barbara—and now deceased. They interested me in the subject, and by interpreters all the Spanish and Indian residents of Santa Barabara in [...] 1853 and previous were interrogated. At that time (1879) the principal actors in the rescue were living. As I sifted the material the doubt arose in my mind as to her the woman being a native Islander. Her manner of meeting her rescuers, was not that of a wild woman, but of one who had knowledge of the amenities of life—Her entrance into white civilized homes, was not that of a creature utterly unfamiliar with house conveniences—her tact in meeting strangers, receiving their gifts politely, and after they were gone—distributing them among the children of her host. Her conversing continuously in a tongue unkown to any—but evidently a language with which she was thoroughly familiar—no hesitation—or forgotten phrases—no one of the various Indian tribes obtainable could understand one word she uttered—A search was made for the people from San Nicholas, but they were scattered over the country, and were never located. They were simply Mission Indians—She told her story by expressive pantomime—and what seemed to be an account of shipwreck—[...]—and looking for rescue—the pictures on the walls of the cave [...]
